name: TypeScript Incremental Compilation
summary: TypeScript's incremental story has two pieces: project references (TS 3.0, 2018) split a codebase into multiple compilation units with explicit dependencies, and `--incremental` (TS 3.4, 2019) writes per-file hash and dependency information into `.tsbuildinfo`. Sub-second rebuild on multi-million-line monorepos.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
TypeScript's incremental compilation has two pieces. **Project references** (TypeScript 3.0, 2018) let a large codebase be split into multiple compilation units with explicit dependencies between them. Each project compiles independently; downstream projects depend on the upstream's emitted declaration files (`.d.ts`) plus a manifest.

**`--incremental`** (TypeScript 3.4, 2019) writes per-file hash and dependency information into `.tsbuildinfo`. On rebuild, files whose hash hasn't changed and whose dependencies haven't changed are skipped. This applies within a single project; combined with project references, it gives sub-second rebuild on multi-million-line monorepos.

sections.title: Limits and trade-offs
sections.content:
The granularity is per-file, not per-symbol. A change to a single function in a 10K-line file invalidates the whole file's compilation; downstream files that depend on the file's `.d.ts` only re-check if the `.d.ts` changes. This is coarser than Salsa-style query-graph invalidation but dramatically simpler to implement.

For TypeScript's design constraints (decade-old codebase, JS-source compilation, browser-target builds), this trade-off is the right one. A query-graph rewrite would be a much larger engineering effort and might not pay off for the typical TypeScript build pattern (build once, run; rebuild on file save). The simpler `.tsbuildinfo` approach captures most of the benefit at much lower cost.
